item.title: “Craving, monks, is a net that widens the wandering, it sticks; because of this craving, this world is destroyed, bound, tangled like a ball of…
item.description:
“Craving, monks, is a net that widens the wandering, it sticks; because of this craving, this world is destroyed, bound, tangled like a ball of thread, like a tangled skein, like muñja grass and babaja reeds, unable to pass beyond the plane of misery, the bad destination, the lower realm, saṃsāra.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 4.199
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, just as a skilled carpenter or his apprentice might use a small peg to knock out, drive out, and remove a larger peg; so…
item.description:
“Monks, just as a skilled carpenter or his apprentice might use a small peg to knock out, drive out, and remove a larger peg; so too, monks, when a monk attends to some sign and gives attention to it, if evil, unwholesome thoughts connected with desire, ill-will, and delusion arise, then that monk should attend to a different sign connected with what is wholesome, not that sign. By attending to a different sign connected with what is wholesome, those evil, unwholesome thoughts connected with desire, ill-will, and delusion are abandoned and subside. With their abandonment, the mind becomes steadied, settled, unified, and concentrated.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 20 – Vitakkasaṇṭhānasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, if that monk, while attending to a different wholesome sign, still has evil, unwholesome thoughts connected with desire, ill-will, and delusion arising, then that…
item.description:
“Monks, if that monk, while attending to a different wholesome sign, still has evil, unwholesome thoughts connected with desire, ill-will, and delusion arising, then that monk should examine the peril in those thoughts: ‘These thoughts are unwholesome, these thoughts are blameworthy, these thoughts lead to painful results.’ By examining the peril in those thoughts, those evil, unwholesome thoughts are abandoned and subside. With their abandonment, the mind becomes steadied, settled, unified, and concentrated.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 20 – Vitakkasaṇṭhānasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Māgandiya, suppose a leprous person with sores and blisters on his limbs, being devoured by worms, scratching the openings of his wounds with his nails,…
item.description:
“Māgandiya, suppose a leprous person with sores and blisters on his limbs, being devoured by worms, scratching the openings of his wounds with his nails, cauterizing his body over a charcoal pit. The more he scratches the wound openings and cauterizes his body, the more those wound openings become impure, foul-smelling, and infected, yet he feels some ease and satisfaction because of the itching of the wound openings. So too, Māgandiya, beings who have not abandoned lust for sensual pleasures, being consumed by craving for sensual pleasures, burned by the fever of sensual pleasures, indulge in sensual pleasures. The more they indulge in sensual pleasures, the more their craving for sensual pleasures increases, and the more they are burned by the fever of sensual pleasures, yet they feel some ease and satisfaction dependent on the five strands of sensual pleasure.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 75 – Māgandiyasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Here, these evil, unwholesome intentions of sensual desire, ill-will, and agitation are a hindrance for the noble disciple in training. Here, monks, a noble disciple…
item.description:
“Here, these evil, unwholesome intentions of sensual desire, ill-will, and agitation are a hindrance for the noble disciple in training. Here, monks, a noble disciple reflects: ‘Sensual pleasures present and future, perceptions of sensual pleasure present and future — both belong to Māra’s realm, Māra’s domain, Māra’s bait, Māra’s food. Suppose I dwell with a mind that is expansive, great, having conquered the world and established the mind’s position. When I dwell with a mind that is expansive, great, having conquered the world and established the mind’s position, then those evil, unwholesome intentions of sensual desire, ill-will, and agitation will not arise; with their abandonment, my mind does not become confined, but rather immeasurable and well-developed.’”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 106 – Āneñjasappāyasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Householder, suppose there is a pit of glowing embers, deeper than a man’s height, filled with glowing embers that are neither flaming nor smoking. Then…
item.description:
“Householder, suppose there is a pit of glowing embers, deeper than a man’s height, filled with glowing embers that are neither flaming nor smoking. Then a man comes who desires to live, not to die, who desires happiness and loathes suffering. Two strong men grab him by both arms and drag him toward that pit of embers. What do you think, householder? Would that man twist his body this way and that?” “Yes, venerable sir. Why is that? Because he knows: ‘If I fall into this pit of embers, I will thereby meet death or deadly suffering.’” “So too, householder, a noble disciple reflects: ‘The Blessed One has said that sensual pleasures are like a pit of embers — of much suffering, much despair, and the peril is even greater.’ Having seen this as it actually is with perfect wisdom … he cultivates such equanimity.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 54 – Potaliyasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Here, monks, a well-taught noble disciple reflects: ‘Now I am being overwhelmed by form. In the past, I was also overwhelmed by form just as…
item.description:
“Here, monks, a well-taught noble disciple reflects: ‘Now I am being overwhelmed by form. In the past, I was also overwhelmed by form just as I am now being overwhelmed by present form. If I were to delight in future form, then in the future I would be overwhelmed by form just as I am now being overwhelmed by present form.’ Reflecting thus, he has no regret for past form, no delight in future form, and practices disenchantment, dispassion, and cessation regarding present form.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.79
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, if one does not intend, does not plan but still has a tendency towards something, that becomes a basis for the establishment of consciousness.…
item.description:
“Monks, if one does not intend, does not plan but still has a tendency towards something, that becomes a basis for the establishment of consciousness. When there is a basis, there is an establishment of consciousness. When consciousness is established and grows, there is the descent of name-and-form. With name-and-form as condition, the six sense-bases … such is the origin of this whole mass of suffering.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 12.39
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, if anyone says: ‘Māra’s complete snare’ — he would speak rightly of women. Indeed, women are ‘Māra’s complete snare.’”
item.description:
“Monks, if anyone says: ‘Māra’s complete snare’ — he would speak rightly of women. Indeed, women are ‘Māra’s complete snare.’”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 5.55
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, the large deep lake refers to sensual pleasures. Monks, the great herd of deer refers to beings. Monks, the man who does not desire…
item.description:
“Monks, the large deep lake refers to sensual pleasures. Monks, the great herd of deer refers to beings. Monks, the man who does not desire their welfare, benefit, security refers to Māra the Evil One. Monks, the dangerous path refers to the wrong eightfold path – wrong view, wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, wrong concentration. Monks, the male decoy refers to delight and greed. Monks, the female decoy refers to ignorance. Monks, the man who desires their welfare, benefit, security refers to the Tathāgata, the Arahant, the Perfectly Enlightened One. The safe, secure path leading to happiness refers to the Noble Eightfold Path – right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 19 – Dvedhāvitakkasutta
📖 Read this sutta
